question: How would you design an offline-first feed for a mobile app?
answer:
Core principle: the UI reads from a LOCAL store and never blocks on network. The network is a refresh source.

Architecture:
• Persistent store — SQLite (Room / SQLDelight / Core Data) keyed by item id.
• Repository — exposes `Flow<List<Item>>` (or AsyncSequence on iOS) backed by the DB.
• Sync engine — background coroutine / WorkManager that pulls fresh data and writes to the DB.
• Pagination — keyset (cursor) pagination preferred over offset, for stable scrolling.
• Cache invalidation — TTL on rows + ETag/`If-None-Match` for conditional fetches.
• Conflict resolution — last-writer-wins or per-field merging. Always include server `updated_at` timestamps.

UI considerations:
• Show stale data with a 'last synced X ago' indicator.
• Pull-to-refresh triggers a sync; UI never loses scroll position.
• Optimistic local writes for user actions (like, save, delete) + outbox to retry server-side.

question: How do you design pagination for a mobile list?
answer:
Two main strategies:

• Offset pagination (`?page=3&limit=20`) — easy server-side; breaks down when items shift (insertions cause duplicates / skips).
• Keyset / cursor pagination (`?after_id=abc&limit=20` or `?after_score=12.3,id=abc`) — stable across inserts; recommended for feeds with frequent updates.

Client-side patterns:
• Persist pages in the local store; UI reads from DB, fetches the next page when the user nears the bottom.
• `androidx.paging` (Android), `Paging` for KMP (Cash App), or hand-rolled `LazyColumn` triggers via `LaunchedEffect(lazyListState)` checks.
• For SwiftUI: `onAppear` on the last `List` row + a coordinator that suppresses concurrent fetches.

UX rules:
• Show skeleton / shimmer while fetching the next page.
• De-dup items by id when merging.
• Cap memory: trim old pages once the user has scrolled far enough; restore on back-scroll if needed.

question: How would you design a chat / messaging feature?
answer:
Chat is one of the hardest mobile features because it combines real-time, offline, attachments, and ordering.

Server transport:
• Long-lived WebSocket (or SSE) for real-time. Auth on connect; reconnect with exponential backoff.
• REST/GraphQL fallback for history fetch and message sending when offline.
• Message ids are server-assigned UUIDs; clients use a temporary `client_id` for optimistic display until server ack.

Client state:
• Local DB stores messages keyed by `id` + `client_id` + `chat_id`. Sort by `(created_at, id)`.
• Outbox: messages with `state in (sending, failed)` retry on connectivity restoration.
• De-dup by `client_id` when server's authoritative copy returns.

Ordering & merging:
• Use server `created_at` for display; tie-break by id.
• Compress duplicate writes (typing indicator at most once/second).
• Handle out-of-order arrival — render messages in created_at order and drop ones whose chat is no longer open.

Misc:
• Push notifications — payload contains chat id + message preview; tap routes to the chat.
• Read receipts — local mark on view, debounced upload to server.
• Attachments — separate upload pipeline with progress; message refers to media url once upload completes.

question: How do you handle modularization and feature flags at scale?
answer:
Modularization (covered separately) gives parallel build + enforced API boundaries. Feature flags add the ability to ship dark code and ramp it.

Flag layers:
• Build-time (BuildConfig / `enum class BuildVariant`) — lowest cost, can't change post-release.
• Remote config (Firebase Remote Config, GrowthBook, ConfigCat, LaunchDarkly, custom) — can flip without a release.
• User-targeted (cohorts, % rollout, A/B test variants).

Discipline:
• Every flag has an owner + an expiry date; track them in a registry / CI lint.
• Default OFF for new features unless declared otherwise.
• Combine flag state with analytics events so you can correlate with product metrics.
• Wrap flag reads behind a typed API (`Flags.checkoutV2Enabled`) — never sprinkle string keys.
• Test BOTH branches in CI; the unused branch is the most likely to bit-rot.

Architectural patterns:
• In multi-module setups, flags can drive WHICH module is wired in. Hilt / Koin can swap implementations based on flag state.
• Combine flags with kill-switches for critical features so production issues can be mitigated without an app update.

question: How do you architect mobile analytics and observability?
answer:
Three pillars:

• Analytics (product) — typed event API, event registry, batched upload, offline buffering. Events go to systems like Mixpanel, Amplitude, Segment, Firebase Analytics.
• Crash reporting — Crashlytics, Sentry, Bugsnag, App Center. Integrate with NDK/iOS native crash handlers.
• Performance / observability — Firebase Performance, Sentry Performance, DataDog mobile RUM, OpenTelemetry mobile SDK. Track startup, frame rate, screen TTI, network call durations.

Design rules:
• Wrap SDKs in a single internal interface — easy to swap providers, easy to test.
• Buffer offline events; flush on connectivity. Cap the buffer size.
• Sample expensive events (long-running spans) to control cost.
• Privacy: never send PII. Use stable anonymous user ids; respect ATT prompt on iOS and the privacy nutrition labels.
• Define event names + properties in a schema (JSON / YAML); generate typed Kotlin/Swift wrappers from it to prevent typos and forgotten properties.

question: Design a system that streams large amounts of data while being kind to the device.
answer:
Big-data scenarios on mobile (live transcript, real-time charts, video streaming, telemetry). Constraints to budget:

• Memory: don't keep the full dataset in RAM. Chunk + paginate; release older chunks.
• Battery: WebSocket / gRPC stream is cheaper than polling; hibernate when app is backgrounded.
• CPU: parse off-main thread; use binary formats (Protobuf / FlatBuffers) over JSON when payload is large.
• Network: gzip/brotli compression; client-side compression for uploads.
• Storage: write to a database with batched transactions; use append-only structures (WAL) for hot writes.
• Backpressure: producer must respect consumer cadence — coroutine `Flow` with `buffer(size, BufferOverflow)` or RxJava backpressure operators.

UI:
• Use windowed views (`LazyColumn` / `RecyclerView`) — only render visible chunks.
• Throttle updates to ≤ 30/sec so the renderer can keep up.
• Show derived stats (mean, last value, sparkline) instead of every frame.

Resilience:
• Reconnect with exponential backoff and jitter; resume from last server offset / sequence number.
• Persist progress periodically so a crash restores state cleanly.
